entries.form_section: In LJLA the Gt infinitive is mostly l)yt(wbd), even though the finite forms show no such waw, presumably a phonetic change (preceding the stressed /ā/ vowel rather than the /i/ or schwa vowel of the finite forms) rather than a separate stem. Note, too, that it is impossible to distinguish pael from peal in the impf. of unvocalized LJLA texts. In Mand. usually written historically ABD unlike the frequent spelling of ˀbd "to be lost" with waw instead of beth
entries.end_notes:
Gt 1.1 see also s.v. bdyˁbd.
Many if not most of the idiomatic constructions of the G stem with a nominal form are calques on similar constructions in Middle Persian. See Ciancaglini, ILS (2013):117.

The apparently causative form אעברת in 1QapGen 26:20 is more likely to be an error caused by the previous series of C forms rather than a passive.
Some of the unusual semantic developments displayed in this root (e.g. "to overwhelm", "become pregnant") may be due to conflation with remnants of the separate Semitic root ḍbr "to heap up"; see note s.v. ṣbr vb.

entries.end_notes: There seems to be confusion as to the proper identification of the constellation, since there are two traditionally viewed as a "wagon" or "chariot(eer)", ie., Charles' Wain (a part of Ursa Major) and Auriga.  Perhaps either could be referenced. Unfortunately, the BH form rendered by the Syriac (מַזָּרוֹת) at Job 38:32 is even more obscure. The cited passage from Jacob, however, only confuses the issue, for the first line seems refer to a constellation that rises with the seasons, thus definitely excluding Charles' Wain, while the second line seems even more clearly to point to a section of Ursa Major -- i.e. it goes round and round the pole and is never unseen.

entries.end_notes:
In OfAEg ˁwd is written with waw but ˁd lˀ without at TAD A404 R.9, so perhaps the spelling of the latter was influenced by that of ˁd lˀ 'not yet' such as at TAD A2.2 R.12 which, since it is used with a preformative verb, like Hebrew טֶרֶם, must be from the conjunction ˁad. Even without the negative adverb, however, there is somewhat of a semantic overlap (and perhaps inter-dialectal confusion?) between the two words. In any case, these spellings suggest a literary basis for the occasional LJLA spelling עד as well.
In early Syriac used where later texts would regularly use ܕܝܢ.

In Arabic this root is used specifically for blinded in one-eye. Where and how frequently this might have been the meaning in Aramaic (as opposed to the more common Aramaic word for "blind" SMY) is not totally clear. The famous cited passage from BR is the only one in the database where one-eyed blindness is clearly expressed without adding an explicit "in one eye." Note that SMY may also be explicitly limited to one eye (see citations there).
See also note s.v. ˁwr#2 vb.

entries.form_section: BA vocalized as (ETA) corresponding to the later forms regularly spelled with internal yodh, but it is likely that it and the other early defectively-spelled forms did not have a long vowel in the first syllable.
entries.end_notes: It has been cogently argued that this word (or a form much like it) also existed in early Syriac (Sirach 23:1; see e.g. W.Th. van Peursen, Language and Interpretation in the Syriac Text of Ben Sira [Brill, 2007], p. 9) then misunderstood by later scribes and converted then by metathesis to ṭˁyw. If correct this would have interesting ramifications for the theories connected Targum and Peshitta, inasmuch as this word (or this root) is not used in the JLA targumic material, only in PTA, LJLA material.

entries.end_notes: Others "upon" is not quite correct, especially for the early examples where the word (rare in Syriac!) tends to be used in place of simple ˁl where the reference is to the space above something. In any case, prior to the late Babylonian dialects, this form, as opposed to ˁl, is only used in reference to physical space, and exceptions in the prints should be considered to be scribal errors. The rare prepositional examples in Syriac seem always to carry the connotation of "protection".

entries.form_section: Mand: Only in secondary forms derived from etpe as if from tar or atr yielding G, hence transitive D: atar; so also one time in JBA.
entries.end_notes:
MD lists forms of this verb s.v. two lemmas "ATR I" 43b and "TAR" 481a. The latter is incorrectly connected there to "ATR II" (i.e. (tr). In Sam. TargA.var. to Lev. 20:23, should be translated as 'to be aware'.
See s.v. ˁrr for possible additional examples of the reduplicated form in JLAtg.
Note that this is one of the middle weak verbs whose non-D T stems we have separated into Gt and Ct depending on the semantics, i.e. on whether it is middle (Gt) or passive/reflexive (Ct).

entries.form_section: In BA past tense narrative this verb (when introducting  <aram>)mr </aram>) is pointed as an active participle wherever possible, but when it is not possible to do so it is pointed as a perfect, which is thus clearly the original form
entries.end_notes:
In early texts always as a part of the expression ˁny w)mr, "to answer saying".
LS2 has created for Syriac a third verb out of whole cloth that is simply a combination of the two Semitic roots "to speak out" and "to be humble". We have here tried to disentangle his mess but at this stage cannot vouch for the proper assignment of lemmas within the CAL's textual databases. B.'s presupposition appears to be that the new verb is strictly a denominative from ˁnyn, though that word is derives from ˁny #1. Note that the native lexicographers do not recognize such a distinction.

The emphatic plural in Syriac means "tens" as opposed to the absolute ˁsryn which is always "twenty." See s.v. ˁsryn num.
In poetic language in Syriac and Mandaic, the "teens" can also be express by "ten and ..." after the manner of the numbers higher than twenty, etc.

entries.end_notes: Azzuz is a common variant in Arabic of the well-known Arabic epithet of Allah, having absorbed an more ancient divinity. Perhaps, though, it reflects instead the Nabatean goddess al-Uzza. Here it is apparently used in reference to a precinct devoted to the worship of that god. See s.v. ˀswr div.

citations.translation: Azziz (var.: Assur) upon whom all throw spittle

entries.end_notes: From BH עַלִּיזִים, but the reference is to the Romans who destroyed the Bar Kochba rebellion with the fall of Beitar; see Jastrow s.v.
